generated_comment:
A: Kaam to sabke liye kaam hi hai, behen. Ab naukri mein baar-baar mahina ka chhutti, phir bachcha hone par lamba leave milega, to daftar wale bhi sochenge na kaun zyada din theek se kaam karega.
B: Haan, par aurat log ko dikkat bhi hota hai.
A: Dikkat to hota hai, par mard ke liye alag se kuchh nahin hai. Aise mein malik bolega aurat rakho to kaam rukega, yahi soch ban jata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames menstruation and maternity-related leave as making women less reliable workers and questions the fairness of accommodations by contrasting them with men. It encodes workplace liability and reverse-discrimination/fairness framing in a casual conversation.
original_frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 39. A Dalit ASHA worker in a rural district in Bihar. You are talking to your neighbour while hanging clothes outside your house. Your neighbour's daughter just got a clerical job at a government office in the nearby town. You are responding and sharing your opinion on whether it is practical for a married woman with children to work outside the home. You also mention that in your experience women like you do not even get the luxury of taking leave when they are unwell because their jobs are not secure enough and their communities do not support it the way upper caste women's families might.
